title: Adjoints of a Geometry
overall_summary: Cheung formalizes an adjoint of a geometric lattice G as a geometric lattice G^A of the same rank into which the inverted lattice G' embeds, with the points of G'—equivalently the copoints of G—mapped onto the points of G^A. The note relates any such adjoint to the lattice E(G) of G-extensions, represented by linear subclasses of copoints or equivalently by modular filters: every flat of G^A determines a linear subclass of G, giving a meet-preserving embedding G^A → E(G). The main obstruction technique is to compute joins of principal linear subclasses inside E(G) via modular filters, and then use semimodularity of the hypothetical geometric adjoint to derive a rank contradiction. The paper applies this to an explicit 8-point geometry and proves that it has no adjoint. For the original tropical problem, the most relevant content is the classical mechanism: an attempted extension of order reversal on flats can be constrained by embedding into an extension lattice and by the behavior of principal extensions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition and stated basic properties of an adjoint
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a geometric lattice, let G' denote the inverted lattice of G, and let copoints mean coatoms of G. An adjoint of G is a geometric lattice G^A of the same rank as G together with a one-to-one order-preserving map e: G' → G^A that maps the points of G' onto the points of G^A. Since the points of G' are the copoints of G, the paper thereafter identifies copoints of G with points of G^A. The paper states that these defining assumptions imply three additional properties of e: e is cover-preserving, e is rank-preserving, and e is meet-preserving, i.e. e(u ∧_{G'} v) = e(u) ∧_{G^A} e(v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The note records these properties immediately after the definition and does not supply a proof. The later proof uses them in a precise way: if two flats of G have a join that covers each of them, then in the inverted lattice G' the corresponding elements cover their meet; cover preservation and meet preservation transfer this to G^A. Rank preservation is then used to know the ranks of the corresponding elements in G^A, so that the top element cannot cover them when the rank gap is larger than one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition: correspondence between linear subclasses and modular filters
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a geometric lattice. A linear subclass C of G is a set of copoints of G such that for any copoints x, y, z of G, if x, y, and z all cover x ∧ y ∧ z, then x, y ∈ C implies z ∈ C. A modular filter M of G is a subset M ⊆ G satisfying: first, if x ∈ M and y ≥ x, then y ∈ M; second, if x, y ∈ M form a modular pair, then x ∧ y ∈ M. The proposition quoted in the paper says: if C is a linear subclass of G, then the set M_C of all elements x of G such that every copoint z ≥ x belongs to C is a modular filter of G; conversely, if M is a modular filter of G, then the set of copoints belonging to M is a linear subclass of G. The paper uses this as the correspondence between G-extensions and linear subclasses or modular filters. A G-extension is called principal if the corresponding modular filter has a least element. The G-extensions, equivalently the linear subclasses ordered by inclusion, form a lattice denoted E(G).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This proposition is quoted from Crapo–Rota and no proof is given in the note. The forward construction packages a linear subclass C as the filter of flats whose containing copoints are all in C. The converse extracts from a modular filter M its copoints; the modular-pair meet axiom is exactly what enforces the linear-subclass closure rule for triples of copoints covering their common meet. Cheung uses the proposition operationally as follows: to show that a generated linear subclass is all copoints, pass to its modular filter M and repeatedly apply the rule that if M contains two modular flats, it contains their meet; once the bottom element is forced into M, upward closure gives M = G, so the corresponding linear subclass is the top element of E(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition: any adjoint embeds meet-preservingly in the extension lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a geometric lattice and let G^A be an adjoint of G. For each flat a of G^A, define C(a) to be the set of copoints H of G such that the corresponding point of G^A lies below a. Then C(a) is a linear subclass of G. Consequently the assignment a ↦ C(a) gives an embedding of G^A into E(G), the lattice of linear subclasses of G ordered by inclusion. This embedding preserves meets.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The points of G^A are identified with the copoints of G. In any geometric lattice, the set of points below a flat is linearly closed: if two points p and q are below a flat a, then the whole line determined by p and q is below a. Under the adjoint identification, line-closure of a set of points of G^A is the same as the linear-subclass condition for copoints of G: whenever copoints x, y, z cover x ∧ y ∧ z, the corresponding points are collinear, so containing two forces containing the third. Thus C(a) is a linear subclass. The map a ↦ C(a) is injective because a flat of a geometric lattice is determined by the set of points below it, and it is order-preserving by construction. Finally, both flats of G^A and linear subclasses in E(G) are closure systems on the same Boolean algebra of copoints of G, with the image of G^A contained in E(G); meets in these closure systems are intersections. Therefore C(a ∧ b) = C(a) ∩ C(b), so the embedding is meet-preserving.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example computation: two principal subclasses generate the top extension in the 8-point geometry
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the 8-point geometry G with points A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H in which the only 4-point planes are ABCD, ABEF, ABGH, CDEF, and CDGH. Let (AB) and (CD) denote the principal linear subclasses associated to the lines AB and CD, and let L be the linear subclass generated by (AB) and (CD). Let M be the modular filter corresponding to L. Then L is the top element 1 of E(G), i.e. L consists of all copoints of G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because L contains the principal subclasses associated to AB and CD, the corresponding modular filter M contains the planes ABEF and ABGH, which contain AB, and also CDEF and CDGH, which contain CD. The planes ABEF and CDEF meet in the line EF, and EF is covered by both planes; in this geometry they form a modular pair, so the modular-filter axiom gives EF ∈ M. Similarly, ABGH and CDGH meet in GH and form a modular pair, so GH ∈ M. The lines EF and GH form a modular pair whose meet is the bottom element 0 of the lattice, so 0 = EF ∧ GH belongs to M. Since M is upward closed, containing 0 forces M = G. Therefore every copoint of G belongs to the linear subclass corresponding to M, so L is the top element of E(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem: the 8-point geometry has no adjoint
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be the 8-point geometry with points A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H whose only 4-point planes are ABCD, ABEF, ABGH, CDEF, and CDGH. Then G has no adjoint in the sense of the paper: there is no geometric lattice G^A of the same rank into which the inverted lattice G' embeds with the points of G' mapped onto the points of G^A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume for contradiction that G has an adjoint G^A. Let x and y be the flats of G^A corresponding, under the embedding of G', to the lines AB and CD of G. In G, the join AB ∨ CD is the plane ABCD, and this plane covers both AB and CD. Therefore, in the inverted lattice G', the elements AB and CD cover ABCD. By the stated cover-preserving and meet-preserving properties of an adjoint embedding, x and y cover x ∧ y in G^A. Now view G^A inside E(G) via the preceding proposition. The example computation shows that the linear subclasses generated by the principal subclasses associated to AB and CD have join 1 in E(G); hence, in the paper's notation, x ∨ y = 1 in E(G). Since the image of G^A is a closure subsystem inside E(G), if the E(G)-join of x and y is already top, then no proper flat in G^A can contain both x and y; hence x ∨ y = 1 also in G^A. But G^A is a geometric lattice and therefore semimodular. In a semimodular lattice, if two elements cover their meet, then their join covers each of them. Thus 1 should cover both x and y. This is impossible: by rank preservation, x and y have the ranks corresponding to the lines AB and CD in the inverted rank-4 lattice, so they have rank 2 in G^A, while the top element has rank 4 and cannot cover rank-2 elements. This contradiction proves that no adjoint exists.

proof_techniques.name: Represent adjoint flats as linear subclasses of copoints
proof_techniques.description: The paper exploits the defining feature of an adjoint: points of G^A are exactly copoints of G. A flat of G^A can therefore be regarded as a subset of the copoints of G, namely the copoints whose corresponding points lie below the flat. Because flats in a geometric lattice are linearly closed sets of points, this subset satisfies the linear-subclass axiom for copoints of G. This gives a canonical embedding of the hypothetical adjoint into E(G), the extension lattice of G.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the tropical question, the natural inclusion of flats into the relative Dressian resembles the inclusion of principal extensions into an extension lattice. A useful strategy is to identify whether tropical linear subspaces inside Trop(M) carry an analogous closure-system or extension-lattice structure, so that any proposed order-reversing extension is forced to interact with principal objects in a rigid way.

proof_techniques.name: Use modular filters to compute joins of generated linear subclasses
proof_techniques.description: Instead of directly manipulating linear subclasses, Cheung passes to the corresponding modular filters. Joins generated by principal subclasses can be detected by showing that the corresponding modular filter must contain more and more lower-rank flats. The key rule is: if a modular filter contains a modular pair x, y, then it contains x ∧ y. In the 8-point example this rule forces ABEF and CDEF to give EF, ABGH and CDGH to give GH, and then EF and GH to give the bottom element, which makes the filter all of G.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A tropical analogue would be to compute the subspace generated by principal tropical objects through a dual or auxiliary structure, rather than by direct containment. If relative Dressians admit a valuated-matroid or extension-theoretic analogue of modular filters, this technique suggests a concrete way to force a generated object to become maximal.

proof_techniques.name: Semimodularity contradiction from a forced top join
proof_techniques.description: The no-adjoint proof combines two independent facts about a pair of elements. First, incidence in G implies that their images x and y in any adjoint must cover x ∧ y. Second, extension-lattice computation implies that their join must be the top element. In a geometric lattice, semimodularity says that if x and y cover their meet, then x ∨ y covers both x and y. If rank preservation makes x and y too low-rank for the top element to cover them, this gives a contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a template for obstruction arguments: find flats F and G whose reversed images should have a local covering relation, but whose associated principal objects in the larger Dressian-like poset generate the whole ambient object. Any additional geometric, ranked, or semimodular structure forced by a proposed extension could then become inconsistent.

proof_techniques.name: Top-forcing transfers even without join preservation
proof_techniques.description: Cheung explicitly notes that the embedding G^A → E(G) need not preserve joins in general. Nevertheless, the proof only needs a special case: if two elements of the sub-closure-system G^A have join equal to the top element in the larger closure system E(G), then their join inside G^A is also top. Indeed, a proper G^A-flat containing both would also be a proper E(G)-linear subclass containing both, contradicting that their E(G)-join is top.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful for the relative Dressian because one may not have well-behaved global join preservation. It may suffice to prove only top-forcing statements for carefully chosen principal tropical subspaces.

proof_techniques.name: Finite incidence configuration as an obstruction certificate
proof_techniques.description: The obstruction is encoded in a small, explicit incidence list: eight points and five specified 4-point planes. The listed planes create two modular intersections yielding lines EF and GH, and those lines then form a modular pair meeting in the bottom element. This finite configuration is enough to certify that two principal extensions generate the top of E(G), which drives the contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests searching among small matroids with prescribed flat-lattice symmetries for analogous finite certificates in the relative Dressian: a small set of flats whose principal tropical subspaces force maximal containment behavior incompatible with an extended duality.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that the embedding G^A → E(G) obtained from an adjoint is not necessarily cover-preserving and not necessarily join-preserving; it says the rank-three geometry of six points in general position exhibits this phenomenon. The cited background sources are Crapo, Orthogonal Representations of Combinatorial Geometries, for the broader polarity/orthogonality motivation; Crapo–Rota, On the Foundations of Combinatorial Theory: Combinatorial Geometries, for descriptions of G-extensions via linear subclasses, modular filters, elementary quotients, and elementary strong maps; and Higgs, A lattice order for the set of all matroids on a set. By the definition in this paper, if a geometric lattice G already has an order-reversing self-isomorphism taking copoints to points, then taking G^A = G gives an adjoint; consequently Cheung's 8-point no-adjoint geometry cannot itself have such a polarity. The paper does not discuss tropical linear spaces or Dressians, but it supplies the classical lattice-extension obstruction pattern that the original problem is tropicalizing.
why_relevant: Introduces adjoints of geometric lattices and relates them to extensions. This is the classical source for the lattice-theoretic polarity problem that the question tropicalizes.
